children.name: Aegotheles
children.description: Small, nocturnal birds that resemble a cross between an owl and a nightjar, with a finely patterned plumage, a slightly hooked bill and a soft, fluttering flight. They are endemic to Australasia and are among the most mysterious of all bird families.

children.name: Aegotheles cristatus
children.description: The most common and widespread owlet-nightjar, found across mainland Australia and Tasmania. It is a small, grey-brown bird measuring about 22 centimetres, with a short tail and a distinctive pale collar. Despite its name, it is more closely related to swifts and hummingbirds than to true nightjars.

children.name: Aegotheles albertisi
children.description: A montane species found in the highland forests of New Guinea at elevations between 1,000 and 3,200 metres. Its plumage is a rich rufous-brown with fine black vermiculations and a mottled breast, providing excellent camouflage against moss-covered branches. It is known for its penetrating, chattering call at dusk.

children.name: Aegotheles crinifrons
children.description: Endemic to the islands of Halmahera and Bacan in the North Maluku region of Indonesia, this species inhabits primary lowland and hill forests. It is larger than other owlet-nightjars with a shaggy, elongated crown and nape feathers. Very little is known about its ecology and behaviour in the wild.

children.name: Aegotheles insignis
children.description: A large owlet-nightjar found in the montane forests of New Guinea at elevations between 1,500 and 3,000 metres. It has rich rufous-brown plumage with fine barring and a distinctive cat-like facial disc. Its call is a series of harsh, grating notes that resemble the mewing of a cat.

children.name: Aegotheles tatei
children.description: A poorly known owlet-nightjar from the montane forests of eastern New Guinea. It has dark brown plumage with small white spots on the crown and wings, giving it a starry appearance. It was only described in 1980 and remains one of the least known members of the family.

children.name: Aegotheles bennettii
children.description: A small owlet-nightjar found in the lowland and foothill forests of New Guinea. It has brown upperparts with bold black barring and a pale belly with fine barring. It is more common in the lowlands than other owlet-nightjars and is often detected by its penetrating, chattering call at dusk.

children.name: Aegotheles wallacii
children.description: Found in the lowland forests of New Guinea and the Aru Islands, named after Alfred Russel Wallace. It has greyish-brown plumage with fine black markings and a pale collar. It is a medium-sized owlet-nightjar with a relatively long tail. Its call is a soft, musical trill.

children.name: Aegotheles archboldi
children.description: A montane owlet-nightjar found in the highlands of New Guinea at elevations above 2,000 metres. It has dark brown plumage with fine buff spots and a relatively short tail. Named after the American explorer Richard Archbold. It is one of the least studied owlet-nightjars, inhabiting remote mossy forests.

children.name: Aegotheles maximus
children.description: This species of Aegothelidae is the largest member of its family, with distinctive plumage and a loud vocalization that can be heard across large parts of Africa.
